---
title: "Beam Lines"
description: "Compose named elements and nested lines into the lattice selected by TRACK."
audience: user
status: current
last-reviewed: "2026-07-23"
---


The accelerator to be studied is represented as a sequence of physical
elements called a beam line. A beam line is built from simpler beam lines whose
definitions can be nested to any level. A `LINE` command provides the formal
definition:

```text
label: LINE = (member, ..., member);
```

Each `member` may be:

- an element label,
- a beamline label,
- a sub-line enclosed in parentheses.

## Simple Beam Lines

The simplest beam line is a list of elements:

```text
label: LINE = (member, ..., member);
```

Example:

```text
L: LINE = (A, B, C, D, A, D);
```

Line attributes:

`ORIGIN`
: position vector of the origin of the line. Elements placed using
  `ELEMEDGE` use this as reference.

`ORIENTATION`
: orientation vector of Tait-Bryan angles at the line origin.

## Sub-lines

Instead of referring directly to an element, a beamline member can refer to
another named beam line. This provides shorthand notation for repeated lattice
segments. Lines and sub-lines can be entered in any order, but when a line is
used, all of its sub-lines must already be known to the parser.

Example:

```text
L: LINE = (A, B, S, B, A, S, A, B);
S: LINE = (C, D, E);
```

The source manual expands this in two steps.

1. Replace `S` by its definition:

```text
(A, B, (C, D, E), B, A, (C, D, E), A, B)
```

2. Omit parentheses:

```text
A, B, C, D, E, B, A, C, D, E, A, B
```
